askNext: What standards are being applied?
Can the person explain why one output is better?
Is this taste, or just familiarity and bias?
Who is the intended audience?
taste.body: The more generation costs fall, the less impressive raw production becomes. Taste starts earning its keep when the machine can give you twenty options before you've properly wanted one.
taste.example: "The model gave ten logo directions. Taste was choosing the only one that did not look like a fintech startup from 2021."
taste.misuse: By treating taste as pure vibes, class signalling, or post-hoc justification for arbitrary preferences. Good taste is not magic. It is trained judgement with standards.
taste.practicalMeaning: Knowing what to keep, what to reject, what to simplify, what to make legible, and what should never have shipped.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A person can distinguish between output that merely exists and output that deserves to survive.

taste.whyExists: Because AI lowers the cost of producing text, images, code, and options. Once output becomes cheap, selection becomes expensive.

askNext: What exact teacher signals are used?
Is the student evaluated on the teacher's failure modes?
When do you refresh the teacher?
teacher-student.body: Teacher-student is a training relationship. Relationships need curricula and tests.
teacher-student.example: "The teacher-student setup transferred style quickly and factuality slowly."
teacher-student.misuse: Stretched into pure metaphor for any mentoring UI. In engineering contexts it should stay tied to training practice.
teacher-student.practicalMeaning: Specify what the teacher provides (logits, sequences, preferences), on which data, and how the student is evaluated against the teacher.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Teacher-student refers to the pairing in knowledge distillation where a larger or stronger teacher model provides signals that train a smaller student model.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Specify what the teacher provides (logits, sequences, preferences), on which data, and how the student is evaluated against the teacher.

teacher-student.warningLabel: Students inherit the teacher's blind spots by default.
teacher-student.whyExists: It is the standard language of distillation setups across deep learning and modern LLM compression.

askNext: Is Telegram the product, or just the distribution layer?
Does the experience rely on bots, Mini Apps, or simple notifications?
Is the choice about user convenience, growth, or avoiding native-app complexity?
telegram.body: Chat interfaces are powerful partly because they lower the activation energy for software. Telegram makes that especially obvious: a bot, a Mini App, and a payment layer can get surprisingly close to "product" before anyone has written a proper mobile app.
telegram.example: "They said they were building an AI product. In practice, they were building a Telegram bot with good timing and a payment hook."
telegram.misuse: By treating Telegram as just a messenger when the real value is often in its bot ecosystem, Mini App surface, lightweight UI layer, and low-friction interaction model. The platform question matters as much as the chat question.
telegram.note: Telegram's current platform docs span bots, Mini Apps, business bots, payments, and attachment-menu integrations. In product conversations, that means Telegram is often less 'channel' than 'deployment shortcut'.
telegram.practicalMeaning: A practical interface layer for AI agents, internal tools, support flows, commerce, alerts, and small web apps that do not need the ceremony of a full native app. Telegram's own docs explicitly position bots and Mini Apps as capable of replacing websites, accepting payments, and handling business workflows.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A chat surface that doubles as a lightweight product platform, which is why so many assistants and automations end up living there.

telegram.whyExists: Because the easiest way to make software feel useful is often to place it where the user already is: inside the chat thread.

askNext: Which tasks actually improve when more inference-time budget is spent?
How is extra compute triggered, measured, and capped?
Are you solving a hard problem, or subsidising a badly specified one?
test-time-compute.body: The mature version of this idea is resource management. The immature version is calling every slow answer thoughtful.
test-time-compute.example: "They added more test-time compute for the thorny cases and promptly discovered that half their workload was only thorny because the prompt was poor."
test-time-compute.misuse: It is misused when greater compute is treated as proof of deeper intelligence rather than as a trade-off. More compute can buy better results on some tasks, but it can also buy slower answers, higher cost, and a longer walk to the wrong place.
test-time-compute.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means setting policies for which requests deserve slower, more expensive handling and which should be answered quickly with a lighter touch. The interesting part is budget allocation, not mystical thinking.

translations.label: research meaning
translations.text: Performance may improve if the system is allowed to work harder at answer time.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Spending more inference-time budget on selected requests to improve results where it is worth the cost.

test-time-compute.whyExists: It exists because performance is not determined only during training. Many systems improve on harder tasks when they are allowed more time, more samples, or more internal work at the moment of answering.

askNext: Which exact model revision are you calling?
Where does fine-tune data reside, and who can access it?
What is the exit plan if pricing or availability shifts?
together-ai.body: Together AI is infrastructure with a catalogue. Judge it like a host, not a prophet.
together-ai.example: "They put the open model on Together AI so finance would stop asking who owned the GPU reservation."
together-ai.misuse: Confused with a model lab, or assumed to make every open model production-ready without evals, scaling work, or data controls.
together-ai.practicalMeaning: Compare model availability, latency, pricing, fine-tune options, and compliance posture against other inference providers for your workload.

translations.label: product meaning
translations.text: Together AI is a platform for running and fine-tuning open models via API and infrastructure services, competing in the inference-provider and model-platform market.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Compare model availability, latency, pricing, fine-tune options, and compliance posture against other inference providers for your workload.

together-ai.warningLabel: A platform is not a model card.
together-ai.whyExists: Open weights need hosts. Platforms package serving, fine-tuning, and model catalogues so teams can ship without owning clusters on day one.

askNext: How many input and output tokens does the workflow typically use?
What is consuming the token budget: user text, retrieval, examples, or structured schemas?
Can the context be shortened without removing information the model genuinely needs?
tokens.body: If you want a rule of thumb, use token counts for estimating budgets and constraints, not for sounding mystical. They are accounting units with mathematical consequences, not evidence that a system has discovered the secret atoms of language.
tokens.example: "The feature failed less because the reasoning was poor and more because the prompt, schema, and four attached policy documents had eaten the token budget."
tokens.misuse: People often say "one token equals one word", which is close enough for a dinner party and wrong enough to break a budget. The mismatch becomes especially annoying in multilingual text, code, JSON, and documents with lots of formatting noise.
tokens.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means three things: how much context you can fit, how long responses take, and how much the request costs. If your prompt, retrieved docs, tool schemas, and expected output all compete for space, tokens are the shared currency.

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: The microscopic line items that add up into very real spend.

tokens.whyExists: Models do not read text the way people do. They operate on tokenised sequences, and limits such as context size, speed, and pricing are usually expressed in tokens rather than words.

askNext: Are you counting input, output, reasoning, tool, and retry tokens the same way across options?
What quality or latency floor must hold before cheaper tokens are allowed to win?
Does the ratio include only model fees, or also serving, egress, eval, and human review?
tokens-per-dollar.body: Tokens per dollar is useful until someone confuses thrift with taste. Count carefully, then decide what you are willing to buy badly.
tokens-per-dollar.example: "Their tokens per dollar looked unbeatable until the evaluation set forced every cheap answer through a second, expensive model."
tokens-per-dollar.misuse: It is treated as quality. A system can mint cheap tokens while still being wrong, slow, unsafe, or expensive once tool calls, retries, long contexts, and human cleanup are counted. Input and output tokens are also not interchangeable currencies.
tokens-per-dollar.practicalMeaning: In practice it means comparing providers and models with the same workload shape, including cache hits, reasoning tokens, tool loops, and failure retries, then deciding whether the cheaper path still meets the quality bar you pretended was fixed.

translations.label: finance meaning
translations.text: How much model traffic you get for each unit of spend.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A rough efficiency score that still needs a quality and latency floor.

tokens-per-dollar.warningLabel: Cheap tokens are not the same as useful answers.
tokens-per-dollar.whyExists: Model choice, routing, caching, and provider pricing all collapse into money. Teams need a crude but shared yardstick when quality claims arrive without invoices attached.

askNext: Does the review inspect live permissions or only the architecture diagram?
Which stale tools, scopes, or credentials were removed last time?
Who owns the risk when access is kept for convenience?
tool-access-review.body: No diagram is attached because the useful mental model is not a flowchart. It is a live access register meeting a person with enough authority to close doors.
tool-access-review.example: "The tool access review found that the calendar agent still had a file-export scope from a prototype nobody wanted to remember."
tool-access-review.misuse: It is reduced to asking whether the team feels comfortable with the current setup. Comfort is not evidence. The review has to inspect real grants, scopes, logs, owners, and revoke paths.
tool-access-review.practicalMeaning: In practice it means inventorying live connectors, checking read and write scopes, confirming user impersonation rules, sampling audit logs, removing unused tools, and giving somebody accountable the power to say no before the agent says "done".

translations.label: governance meaning
translations.text: Check what the agent can actually touch, then remove what no longer earns its access.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Permission hygiene with receipts, owners, logs, and the occasional awkward revocation.

tool-access-review.whyExists: Tool access drifts. Prototypes gain connectors, pilots inherit credentials, teams add exceptions, and the original permission story quietly becomes folklore. Review exists to compare the declared design with the live system.

askNext: Which tools are allowed for this exact workflow, not the platform in general?
Does the allowlist distinguish read, draft, write, send, and delete operations?
Who can add tools to the list, and how is stale access removed?
tool-allowlist.body: An allowlist is useful because it is boring. The moment it becomes aspirational, the agent has already begun negotiating with production.
tool-allowlist.example: "The support agent could read orders and draft refunds, but the tool allowlist kept the actual refund tool behind a separate approval gate."
tool-allowlist.misuse: It gets treated as a static checkbox. A generous allowlist with broad write access, production credentials, and no review cadence is not a control. It is an invitation with better formatting.
tool-allowlist.practicalMeaning: In practice it means named tools, allowed operations, read and write distinctions, environment boundaries, user-specific scopes, approval thresholds, and a review process for removing access once the prototype has stopped pretending to be temporary.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: Only these tools may be called, and only in these ways.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A permission boundary that needs maintenance, not a screenshot from the launch checklist.

tool-allowlist.whyExists: Agents become safer and easier to reason about when they cannot reach every integration by default. An allowlist turns "the model has tools" into a narrower claim about which tools are available and under which conditions.

askNext: Which approvals actually require judgement, and which are workflow noise?
Does the reviewer see enough context to block the action intelligently?
How often does the system ask for approval, and what happens to attention after repetition?
tool-approval-fatigue.body: No diagram is attached because the failure is behavioural. The system may show a tidy loop; the person inside it is still human.
tool-approval-fatigue.example: "After the fiftieth calendar lookup, the reviewer approved the file-sharing request too, which is how tool approval fatigue earned its own incident note."
tool-approval-fatigue.misuse: Teams use approval prompts as proof that a human is in the loop. Often the human is only in the way. If the reviewer cannot distinguish trivial calls from dangerous ones, the approval surface becomes camouflage for weak automation design.
tool-approval-fatigue.practicalMeaning: In practice it means risk-tiered approvals, batched requests, clear summaries, hard stops for irreversible actions, quiet handling for safe routine work, and escalation paths that respect the fact that vigilance is a scarce resource.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: Repeated approvals degrade into habit unless the system protects attention.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A human-in-the-loop design that converted judgement into reflex.

tool-approval-fatigue.warningLabel: A tired approver is not a control surface.
tool-approval-fatigue.whyExists: Human approval is a real control for risky actions. It fails when the workflow turns every minor step into an interruption and then acts surprised that the reviewer stops reading.

askNext: Who decides which tools are visible in this context: the agent, the broker, or a policy layer above both?
How are permissions, tenancy, and environment boundaries enforced before a tool is exposed?
What happens when the broker itself routes the agent to the wrong capability?
tool-broker.body: Tool brokers become interesting the moment they start saying no. Exposing every tool to every agent is not flexibility. It is future incident material with an API.
tool-broker.example: "The tool broker was the quiet adult in the room. It let the agent discover the search tool and blocked the urge to improvise with production billing."
tool-broker.misuse: It gets described as though brokering itself guarantees security or good judgement. It does not. A broker can make tool access more governable, but it can also become a fashionable wrapper around the same old permission mistakes if the policy behind it is sloppy.
tool-broker.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means registries, capability filters, tenancy rules, environment-aware allowlists, protocol adapters, and a decision point that determines whether the agent gets a calculator, a search index, a payment action, or a polite refusal.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: A controlled gatekeeper between the agent and the set of external capabilities.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The layer deciding which tools are visible and callable before the agent gets ideas.

tool-broker.whyExists: Agents rarely need unrestricted visibility of every available capability. A broker exists to separate tool exposure from tool implementation, making it easier to apply permissions, environment boundaries, capability filtering, or contextual access rules.

askNext: Can we see which tool was called, with which arguments, and under whose authority?
Are sensitive values redacted without destroying the evidence needed for review?
Can the audit connect tool calls to user-visible outcomes and incidents?
tool-call-audit.body: Tool call audits are dull until they are missing. Then they become the most exciting document no one wrote.
tool-call-audit.example: "The tool call audit showed that the agent had not gone rogue; it had simply been granted a calendar tool, a vague goal, and the confidence of a junior consultant."
tool-call-audit.misuse: It gets reduced to raw logs nobody can interpret. A useful audit does not merely prove that an endpoint was hit. It shows whether the call was authorised, expected, correctly formed, successful, and tied to an accountable workflow.
tool-call-audit.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means structured logs, argument capture, result summaries, redaction policy, actor identity, permission state, idempotency markers, and enough retention to investigate incidents without storing every secret forever.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: A record of external actions good enough to review after the applause or the incident.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The receipt for model-powered side effects.

tool-call-audit.whyExists: Tool use moves AI systems from text generation into action. Once a model can query data, write files, send messages, or change records, the organisation needs a way to inspect what was called and what happened next.

askNext: Which tools are read-only, and which can change external state?
What actions require human approval before execution?
How are permissions enforced technically rather than merely described in a prompt?
tool-permissions.body: Tool permissions matter because the most expensive model mistake is often not the wrong sentence. It is the wrong action.
tool-permissions.example: "The assistant seemed sensible until they realised its tool permissions allowed it to email customers directly, which was brave in precisely the wrong way."
tool-permissions.misuse: The term gets flattened into a yes-or-no question, as if "has tools" were the whole story. Real permissions are granular: read versus write, staging versus production, local versus external, draft versus send, observe versus act.
tool-permissions.practicalMeaning: In practice it means allowlists, approval gates, budget limits, environment boundaries, impersonation rules, audit logs, and hard stops around actions with irreversible consequences. If the model can act, the permission model is part of the product.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A map of what the model may read, write, send, and break before a human intervenes.

tool-permissions.warningLabel: A prompt that says 'be careful' is not a permission system.
tool-permissions.whyExists: They exist because once a model can do more than answer questions, it can also create tickets, write files, query production systems, or send messages somebody will later have to explain. Capability without permission design is merely incident planning in civilian clothes.

askNext: Where do tool descriptions, schemas, and permissions come from, and who can change them?
Can the agent verify a tool's identity and scope before invoking it?
What stops a third-party integration from advertising capabilities it should never have?
tool-poisoning.body: The software industry spent years learning that configuration is attack surface. Agent systems have helpfully rediscovered the lesson in prose.
tool-poisoning.example: "The agent did not get hacked by sorcery; it was simply handed a poisoned tool description and obeyed it with enterprise enthusiasm."
tool-poisoning.misuse: It should not be used for every bad tool output. The more precise issue is corruption of the tool interface itself: the agent is misled about what the tool is, what it does, or how it should be used.
tool-poisoning.practicalMeaning: In practice it means malicious MCP descriptors, misleading plugin metadata, tampered schemas, or tool naming collisions that redirect the agent into unsafe actions, data leakage, or expensive nonsense.

translations.label: security meaning
translations.text: An attacker has manipulated the agent's view of a tool rather than the tool's code alone.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The agent is trusting compromised tool metadata and making unsafe calls because of it.

tool-poisoning.warningLabel: The tool layer is part of the prompt whether engineering admits it or not.
tool-poisoning.whyExists: Modern agents do not merely call tools; they read about them. If the model relies on tool descriptions to decide what is safe, relevant, or authoritative, poisoning that layer becomes a prompt injection problem with better infrastructure.

askNext: Who owns each registered tool and its schema?
Which permissions travel with the tool, and which are granted per task?
How does the registry retire dangerous or stale tools before agents keep calling them out of nostalgia?
tool-registry.body: A good tool registry is less like an app store and more like a key cabinet. The labels matter, and so does the lock.
tool-registry.example: "The tool registry made the agent less adventurous by removing three obsolete endpoints and one payroll action nobody remembered approving."
tool-registry.misuse: It gets treated as a plugin marketplace with fewer consequences. Registering a tool does not make it safe, maintained, authorised, or suitable for an agent that has just been persuaded by a malicious PDF.
tool-registry.practicalMeaning: In practice it means schema versioning, permission boundaries, ownership, environment separation, audit logs, deprecation rules, and a process for deciding which tools an agent sees in a given task.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: Keep the agent's available actions discoverable, typed, owned, and governed.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A catalogue that becomes security-critical the minute a model can act on it.

tool-registry.whyExists: Agents need a reliable way to know which actions are available and how to call them. As tool ecosystems grow, teams need registries to avoid stale schemas, duplicated integrations, hidden permissions, and the old tradition of guessing from a README last touched during a launch week.

askNext: Does this tool fail clearly enough for the caller to retry, escalate, or stop?
Are schemas, side effects, and permissions stable across versions and environments?
What happens when the tool times out halfway through an action that matters?
tool-reliability.body: Tool reliability rarely headlines the launch deck, which is precisely why it gets to decide whether the launch keeps working a week later.
tool-reliability.example: "The model was blamed for the bad refund flow until someone noticed the payment tool treated duplicate requests as a creative writing exercise."
tool-reliability.misuse: It gets reduced to uptime or successful HTTP responses. A tool can be "up" and still be unreliable because it returns partial state, inconsistent schemas, non-idempotent side effects, or errors too vague for the caller to recover from intelligently.
tool-reliability.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means schema stability, timeouts, retries, idempotency, versioning, error semantics, sandbox boundaries, and the quiet labour of making external actions predictable enough that a model can call them without inventing theology around transient failure.

translations.label: engineering meaning
translations.text: The tool behaves predictably enough that the caller does not need to hallucinate its failure mode.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A tool that can be called repeatedly without turning ordinary automation into incident archaeology.

tool-reliability.whyExists: An agent is only as dependable as the tools it calls. Even strong reasoning degrades rapidly when the surrounding tools are flaky, under-specified, slow, or capricious about their outputs.

askNext: Can we inspect the raw tool result separately from the model's explanation?
Does the system keep errors and partial results, or only successful summaries?
Which downstream decisions rely on the tool result rather than the generated prose?
tool-result.body: A tool result is where the transcript briefly stops being literature. Preserve that moment.
tool-result.example: "The tool result said the account was inactive; the assistant's summary said the user was probably ready to upgrade, which was generous and entirely unhelpful."
tool-result.misuse: It gets treated as though the model's summary and the tool's output are the same thing. They are not. A neat paragraph can misread a correct payload, hide a warning, or politely bury a failed call.
tool-result.practicalMeaning: In practice it means preserving raw outputs, errors, timestamps, identifiers, and schemas long enough for the system to explain what happened after the model has finished being fluent.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The tool's returned payload, not the assistant's tidied-up story about it.

tool-result.whyExists: Agents and assistants need to distinguish between what an external system actually returned and what the model later says about it. That boundary matters for debugging, audits, citations, retries, and blame.

askNext: What exact meaning of Tool schema is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
tool-schema.body: Tool schema gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Tool Registry keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
tool-schema.example: "Once tool schema entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
tool-schema.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
tool-schema.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

tool-schema.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: What metadata is indexed and how is search quality evaluated?
Are permissions applied before discovery as well as before execution?
What is the fallback when the needed tool is not retrieved?
tool-search-tool.body: The enterprise first accumulated thousands of capabilities. It then invented search so the agent could experience the same onboarding problem as everyone else.
tool-search-tool.example: "The agent searched three thousand tools and found the correct one, leaving only the smaller matter of whether it should call it."
tool-search-tool.misuse: It is marketed as a cure for tool sprawl when it is chiefly an index over the sprawl. Poor names, overlapping capabilities, weak descriptions, and unsafe tools remain poor after retrieval; they are simply discovered more efficiently.
tool-search-tool.practicalMeaning: Mark some tool definitions for deferred loading, expose a search mechanism, and let the model retrieve a small relevant subset. Evaluate recall, selection accuracy, latency, permission filtering, and what happens when the correct tool is described in the wrong corporate dialect.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Retrieve a few tool schemas when needed instead of charging every request rent for the entire catalogue.

tool-search-tool.warningLabel: Discovery quality becomes part of tool reliability.
tool-search-tool.whyExists: Large tool catalogues consume context and make selection less reliable. Just-in-time discovery keeps the initial prompt smaller and presents the model with a focused set of definitions for the current task.

askNext: Which tools are redundant, stale, or too broadly permissioned for the tasks they serve?
Who owns each schema and deprecates it before agents keep using it out of habit?
Can the agent discover only the tools relevant to the current task, or is it browsing a dangerous buffet?
tool-sprawl.body: Tool sprawl is what happens when capability is measured by addition and nobody budgets for subtraction.
tool-sprawl.example: "The assistant had twenty-seven tools, nine ways to read a document, and exactly one adult in the room asking why."
tool-sprawl.misuse: It gets confused with having many tools. Quantity alone is not the sin. Sprawl begins when duplication, stale schemas, hidden permissions, and overlapping functions make the system broader than the organisation can actually reason about.
tool-sprawl.practicalMeaning: In practice it means agents seeing near-duplicate tools, calling the wrong one, failing on inconsistent schemas, inheriting permissions nobody revisited, and forcing operators to discover that discoverability is itself a security question.

translations.label: platform meaning
translations.text: The tool surface is expanding faster than the organisation's ability to govern it.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful stack slowly becoming a confusing and security-relevant cupboard.

tool-sprawl.whyExists: Tool use is where AI systems become useful, so teams keep adding integrations. The trouble arrives when each new connector solves a local problem while making the overall capability surface harder to govern, test, secure, or even describe accurately.

askNext: Which tools are available, and what can each one affect?
What happens when a tool returns stale data, partial failure, or nonsense?
Is the system using tools to extend capability, or to mask weak product design?
tool-use.body: Tool use is where the modern AI stack becomes refreshingly concrete. Once an external system is involved, rhetoric gives way to permissions, logs, and consequences.
tool-use.example: "The assistant became materially more useful once tool use let it search the docs, check the account, and stop inventing the answer from memory."
tool-use.misuse: By marketing tool access as autonomy rather than integration. The hard part is not that the model can ask for a tool. The hard part is deciding which tools it may touch, when, and under whose supervision.
tool-use.practicalMeaning: In practice it means schemas, permission scopes, retries, observability, error handling, and a constant negotiation between flexibility and the desire to avoid discovering new incident classes at scale.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The model gets more useful by leaning on ordinary software, and more dangerous if nobody governs the relationship.

tool-use.whyExists: Because models know many things badly, some things approximately, and almost nothing about the current state of your systems unless another tool tells them.

askNext: What can this tool actually read, write, send, delete, or spend?
Where is model intent validated before it becomes external action?
Which failure would be expensive even if the generated text looked reasonable?
tool-layer-risk.body: Tool-layer risk is where product enthusiasm becomes a systems problem. Once the tool can act, the model's mistake is only the opening sentence.
tool-layer-risk.example: "The model merely chose the refund tool; tool-layer risk arrived when that tool could issue real payments without checking account policy."
tool-layer-risk.misuse: It is misread as another flavour of hallucination risk. A perfectly plausible tool call can still be unsafe if the tool has the wrong scope, stale context, weak validation, or authority the task never deserved.
tool-layer-risk.practicalMeaning: In practice it means mapping every tool to the data it can read, the state it can change, the external parties it can contact, and the controls that stop a bad call from becoming an incident with a timestamp.

translations.label: operator meaning
translations.text: The risk created by what connected tools are allowed to do after the model has asked.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: The incident surface moves from text generation to permissions, validators, logs, and irreversible side effects.

tool-layer-risk.warningLabel: A safe answer can still trigger an unsafe action.
tool-layer-risk.whyExists: Model output is not always the final hazard. In agentic systems, the serious consequence often appears when a model's structured request reaches ordinary software with real permissions.

askNext: What exact meaning of Trace replay is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?

trace-replay.body: Trace replay gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Agent Ops keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
trace-replay.example: "Once trace replay entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
trace-replay.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
trace-replay.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

askNext: What exact meaning of Traffic shaping is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
traffic-shaping.body: Traffic shaping gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around Model Router keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
traffic-shaping.example: "Once traffic shaping entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
traffic-shaping.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
traffic-shaping.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

traffic-shaping.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: Are we talking about pretraining, fine-tuning, or some lighter adaptation method?
What data is being used, and how is quality being checked?
What changed in the weights, and how are you verifying the change was worth it?
training.body: One of the most useful distinctions in AI is simple: training changes the model; inference uses it. Many confused product conversations improve immediately once that sentence is said aloud.
training.example: "The team was not training a new model from scratch; they were fine-tuning an existing one and trying not to call both things innovation."
training.misuse: It gets used as if any change to a model counts as training. Not quite. Prompting is not training. Calling an API is not training. Even many "learning" product features are really retrieval, memory, or rule changes layered around a frozen model.
training.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means data pipelines, filtering, labelling, loss curves, GPUs, checkpoints, failed runs, parameter updates, evaluation cycles, and weeks or months of engineering effort. It is one of the least glamorous ways to spend a lot of money.

translations.label: investor meaning
translations.text: A sign that the company sounds more foundational than it probably is.

translations.label: engineer meaning
translations.text: The place where data quality and optimisation choices come back to judge you.

training.whyExists: The term exists because models do not arrive preloaded with competence. Their behaviour is shaped by exposure to data, objective functions, optimisation choices, and huge amounts of compute.

askNext: What exact meaning of Training signal quality is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
training-signal-quality.body: Training signal quality gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around RLVR keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
training-signal-quality.example: "Once training signal quality entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
training-signal-quality.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.
training-signal-quality.practicalMeaning: In practice it usually means slowing the conversation down enough to ask whether the label points to a real system property, a commercial incentive, a user behaviour, or just a familiar rhetorical flourish.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A useful nearby term, provided someone explains the mechanism instead of using it as scenery.

askNext: What exact meaning of Transformer is being used here?
What changes operationally if this label is removed from the sentence?
Is this naming a mechanism, a vendor choice, a risk, or a wish?
transformer.body: Transformer gets its own page because the surrounding discussion around GPT keeps needing this sharper distinction. Used well, it turns a vague AI-adjacent gesture into a claim someone can test, budget for, or push back on.
transformer.example: "Once transformer entered the discussion, the team finally had a name for the thing everyone had been gesturing at with less precision."
transformer.misuse: It gets abused when people use the phrase as a mood, a shortcut, or a status signal instead of explaining the actual mechanism, incentive, or trade-off they mean.

transformer.whyExists: It exists because the main term alone is often too blunt. The surrounding conversation needs a way to name the neighbouring idea without pretending it is the same thing.

askNext: What evaluates partial thoughts?
What are depth/breadth limits?
Does a simpler verifier plus best-of-n suffice?
tree-of-thoughts.body: Tree of Thoughts is deliberate exploration. Deliberation should be budgeted.
tree-of-thoughts.example: "Tree of Thoughts found a clever plan on branch seven and a cloud bill on every branch."
tree-of-thoughts.misuse: Applied to trivial tasks where the tree mostly spends tokens. Evaluation heuristics can be as wrong as the thoughts they score.
tree-of-thoughts.practicalMeaning: Use when intermediate states are scoreable and the search budget is worth it. Log branches for debugging; cap depth and breadth hard.

translations.label: technical meaning
translations.text: Tree of Thoughts (ToT) is a prompting and search framework that explores multiple intermediate reasoning branches, evaluating partial thoughts and expanding promising paths rather than following a sin

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: Use when intermediate states are scoreable and the search budget is worth it.

tree-of-thoughts.warningLabel: Search multiplies cost faster than insight.
tree-of-thoughts.whyExists: Some problems need lookahead, backtracking, and exploration. Linear chain-of-thought is a single trajectory gamble.

askNext: Where does untrusted content enter the system?
Which permissions or assumptions change at this boundary?
What validation happens before information crosses it?
trust-boundary.body: Trust boundaries are easiest to see when they were absent. This is not the recommended discovery method.
trust-boundary.example: "The web page crossed a trust boundary when it became model context, which is why its instruction to email the database should not have been treated as strategy."
trust-boundary.misuse: It gets drawn after the incident, usually with admirable clarity and no effect on the previous architecture.
trust-boundary.practicalMeaning: In practice it means separating trusted and untrusted content, constraining tools, validating inputs, limiting data movement, and refusing to let one component's confidence become another component's permission.

translations.label: what it usually means in reality
translations.text: A line where validation and permissions must change, not a diagram someone shows after the breach.

trust-boundary.whyExists: AI systems combine user input, retrieved content, tool outputs, model text, and external services. Boundaries identify where validation, permissions, sanitisation, and audit controls need to change.

askNext: What identity and authority does the credential prove, and who vouches for it?
How are credentials scoped, rotated, revoked, and protected against replay?
Which merchant decision changes after verification succeeds?
trusted-agent-protocol.body: No built-in diagram is attached because the missing mental model is credential issuance, signature verification, and merchant policy, not a general agent action loop.
trusted-agent-protocol.example: "TAP confirmed that the visitor was a recognised shopping agent, leaving the merchant to decide whether it should be allowed near the refund policy."
trusted-agent-protocol.misuse: The word "trusted" is easily promoted from authenticated to benevolent. A valid agent identity does not prove that the user approved this purchase, that the recommendation was sound, or that the merchant should permit every requested action.
trusted-agent-protocol.practicalMeaning: Participating agents attach signed credentials or request evidence that merchants can verify and map to policy. Deployment still requires user consent, authorisation limits, replay protection, key rotation, fraud controls, audit trails, and a decision about which issuers the merchant trusts.

translations.label: merchant meaning
translations.text: Verify which commerce agent is calling, then apply a policy that remains your responsibility.

trusted-agent-protocol.warningLabel: Recognised identity is not blanket authority.
trusted-agent-protocol.whyExists: Web shops traditionally infer trust from browser sessions, user actions, accounts, and fraud signals. Agents disturb those assumptions by browsing and buying on someone's behalf, so merchants need stronger evidence about the agent, its operator, and the authority attached to a request.
